column1: A cross-modal high-resolution image generation approach based on cloud-terminal collaboration for low-altitude intelligent network
column3: Cross-modal high-resolution image generation for Low Altitude Intelligent Networking (LAIN)
column4: SEN1-2 dataset (22,500 training pairs, 2,625 test pairs)
column5: DR-MFGAN (Downscaling Reconstruction Multi-scale Locally Focused GAN), based on CycleGAN
column6: Wavelet transform (Morlet wavelet, VisuShrink thresholding) applied for downscaling, denoising, and cleaning in a dedicated Zero-shot Downscaling Reconstruction Module (ZDRM)
column7: Speckle noise: PSNR >5% above baselines; MSE reduced by 18.18%, PSNR increased by 33.52%, LPIPS improved by 39.54%; input image quantitative metrics: PSNR up to 16.258, SSIM up to 0.151, LPIPS down to 0.454, MSE down to 5507.97, HIST up to 0.704 on SEN1-2
column8: Achieved high-quality, robust, and precise cross-modal image generation from SAR to visible light, especially at edge nodes with limited data; surpassed baselines in denoising and image generation; supports latency-sensitive missions in LAIN.
column9: Integrated zero-shot downscaling denoising (ZDRM) with wavelet transform, multi-scale residual learning (MRL) using Res2Net modules, TripletAttention for cross-channel local focusing, and federated learning for cloud-edge collaboration.
column10: Demonstrated strong robustness with federated learning: model performs well with limited/noisy data on edge devices, stable convergence after multiple weight integrations, and comparable performance to original models trained on full data; sensitive to extremely high noise or small/specialized datasets.
column11: Limitations: Fluctuating loss function during federated learning when data is scarce or non-uniform; generalization beyond SAR-RGB needs further validation (e.g., infrared, point cloud); federated learning paradigm could benefit from improved global aggregation methods; desert image color bias due to few samples.
column12: Adversarial (GAN) loss, cycle consistency loss (L1), feature matching loss, residual and consistency loss in denoising module.
column13: Unsupervised for image translation; federated (distributed) learning for model updates; no labels required for denoising; local training on edge with aggregated updates in cloud.
column14: CycleGAN (unsupervised image-to-image translation); customized into DR-MFGAN and MFGAN variants.
column15: Generator: Res2Net-based with multi-scale residual blocks, TripletAttention (encoder), Conv-InstanceNorm-LeakyReLU (CIL); Decoder: ReflectionPad2d + CIL; Discriminator: not explicitly specified (classic PatchGAN structure implied).
column16: Wavelet transform and downscaling cleaning in Zero-shot Downscaling Reconstruction Module (prior to generator); TripletAttention in generator encoder.
column17: Comprehensive comparisons: Outperforms BM3D, NB2NB, ZS-N2N for denoising; exceeds DivCo-DRIT, Pix2Pix, starGAN, U-gat-it, NICE-GAN in fusion/generation; ablation shows CLF, MRB, and ZDRM modules increase all metrics.
column18: Input images resized to 512×512 pixels, generator input 256×256×3 (internal resolution up to 262×262×3).
column19: Baselines: BM3D, NB2NB, ZS-N2N, convolutional neural networks, transformers, GANs (Pix2Pix, DivCo-DRIT, starGAN, U-gat-it, NICE-GAN), and ablations.
column20: Composed of downscaling module (convolutional kernels for image pair splitting), wavelet transform, VisuShrink thresholding, symmetric residual learning blocks (Res2Net), attention blocks (TripletAttention), encoder-decoder with Conv, InstanceNorm, LeakyReLU, ReflectionPad2d; nine multi-scale residual blocks between encoder and decoder.
column22: Tested on SAR-to-RGB only; generalization to other modalities and datasets stated as future work; desert images with limited samples result in color bias.
column23: Discusses reduced time/data cost and local training on resource-constrained Jetson AGX Xavier devices with batch size 1; improved scalability with federated learning for multiple edge nodes; cloud-server aggregation enables parallelism.
column24: SGD optimizer with momentum 0.7, weight decay 6×10⁻⁴, initial learning rate 0.2; batch sizes (training: 4 in main experiments, 1 on Jetson edge devices); learning rate reduced on plateau.
column25: Reports stable convergence trends with federated learning after ~10 rounds; loss stabilization with weight aggregation; minor instability when data is extremely scarce, but overall robust; regularization assisted by residual learning and attention; no explicit mention of dropout or similar regularization.
column26: Highlights feature extraction improvement via multi-scale residual modules (Res2Net), cross-channel and spatial feature aggregation (TripletAttention), explicit wavelet-frequency feature extraction, and local/global attention; improved retention of details, local regions, and noise suppression.

column11: No explicit shortcomings discussed; some mention of possible future research directions and inherent challenges in fine detail preservation

column13: Supervised training

column16: Wavelet transform in encoder before last convolution; inverse wavelet transform before first decoder layer

column19: Compared against ACFM, DMCR, DMCG, WSKN

column24: AdamW used for optimization for both generator and discriminator
column25: CNN regularization via skip connections and multi-scale modules; ablation studies confirm effective convergence and improved stability with wavelet/multi-scale modules
column26: Multi-scale feature extraction (dilated convolutions), Haar wavelet features, auxiliary features (normals, albedo, depth) for richer representation

column1: Adaptive Image Compression Using GAN based Semantic-perceptual Residual Compensation
column4: ImageNet (30,000 images for training), Kodak PhotoCD (24 images for testing)
column5: GAN-based Dense Auto-encoder (GDAE), U-shaped encoder-decoder
column6: Strip pooling in encoder and decoder, dense residual connections, soft quantization (vector), Grad-CAM-based semantic heat maps
column7: PSNR: 33.8, MS-SSIM (dB): 20.1 (Kodak at ~0.5bpp), Run Time: 912ms (GDAE+SPRC)
column8: Achieves state-of-the-art or better visual quality at low bitrate; better PSNR, MS-SSIM, and sharper details compared to BPG, JPEG, JPEG2000, Balle's, and Lee's methods; effective semantic-aware compression
column9: Design of semantic-perceptual residual compensation block (SPRC) combined with dense residual connections and strip pooling in a GAN-based framework; adaptive content-aware regional compression
column10: Ablation and comparative results show strong PSNR, MS-SSIM; semantic compensation modules improve structure and details; use of large, diverse ImageNet and unseen Kodak set
column11: Challenges in semantic interpretation, higher computation time due to SPRC/BPG, semantic block is reliant on VGG, not tested beyond Kodak/ImageNet, possible scalability/future work implied
column12: Combined weighted loss: adversarial (GAN), MSE, SSIM (weighted 10:5:2); sometimes ablation without one; Binarizer regularization included
column13: Unsupervised training task (no labels, only input images used)
column15: Discriminator with fully connected layers; generator (decoder) uses upsampling with dense residual blocks; encoder is downsampling with residuals and strip pooling
column16: Pooling (strip pooling) embedded within encoder and decoder; Grad-CAM applied via VGG branch at front end for SPRC module
column17: Compared against JPEG, JPEG2000, BPG, Balle's, Lee's, and ablations of their own model; GDAE+SPRC yields superior scores; full ablation table included
column18: Not specified; Kodak images of different sizes and contents"
column19: blocks of 16×16 for SPRC but not input resolution"
column20: JPEG, JPEG2000, BPG, Balle’s 2018, Lee’s 2019, baseline auto-encoder
column21: Encoder and decoder: Conv-BN-LeakyReLU stacks, strip pooling, dense residual connect paths (6 in encoder, repeated in decoder), upsampling with deconv; custom quantizer; VGG-16 (for SPRC)
column22: Not specified
column23: Tested Kodak after ImageNet training; demonstrates generalization from ImageNet to Kodak
column24: Training/inference times, BPG step is time-consuming; computation on a dual RTX2080Ti GPU; code optimization or high-res scalability not discussed
column25: SGD optimizer, momentum 0.95, poly learning rate decay (power 0.6), initial LR 0.0005, batch size 32
column26: Reported no stability issues; regularization via binarizer loss; stable convergence with weighted loss; GAN does not affect inference time
column27: Extract explicit convolutional, semantic (VGG, Grad-CAM) feature maps; semantic heat maps define ROI for adaptive compression

column1: An Investigation of Time-Frequency Representation Discriminators for High-Fidelity Vocoder
column4: LibriTTS, LJSpeech, VCTK, M4Singer, PJS, Internal Dataset (CN), OpenSinger, PopCS, Opencpop, CSD

column7: Singing voice MOS: up to 3.80, FPC: 0.972, F0RMSE: 36.137, PESQ: 3.040 (HiFi-GAN+S+C+W). Speech MOS: up to 4.19, FPC: 0.882, F0RMSE: 178.665, PESQ: 3.257. NSF-HiFiGAN/BigVGAN/APNet also improved with MS-SB-CQT/CWT/STFT. Preference gains, e.g., BigVGAN +S+C+W: 84.21% seen, 95.74% unseen.
column8: Proposed MS-SB-CQT and MS-TC-CWT discriminators improve synthesis quality and pitch/phase modeling; joint TFR-based discriminators further boost performance and generalization.
column9: Joint TFR-based discriminators demonstrated improved robustness and generalization across speakers, singers, and multiple GAN vocoders. Enhanced unseen case performance.
column10: CQT-based discrimination may degrade generalization for non-musical signals (speech); APNet remains challenged on phase modeling. General computational/memory costs; further optimization/future work possible.

column16: Direct comparison with HiFi-GAN, HiFi-GAN (+S/C/W/S+C+W), NSF-HiFiGAN, BigVGAN, APNet; ablations (w/o modules); demonstrates state-of-the-art or improved results

column4: DIV2K (training), Set5, Set14, BSD100, Urban100, DIV2K (validation)

column6: Stationary Wavelet Transform (SWT), sym7 wavelet; SWT-domain losses applied on Y-channel for loss computation (fidelity, adversarial); discriminator operates on HF SWT subbands; losses weighted per subband and level; experiments also with different wavelet families and 2 decomposition levels
column7: Set5: PSNR 31.334, SSIM 0.866, LPIPS 0.065, DISTS 0.110, NIQE 4.175, PI 3.160, LR-PSNR 49.911, NRQM 8.252; Similarly strong numbers on Set14, BSD100, Urban100, DIV2K; Outperforms comparable GAN-SR approaches on perception-distortion trade-off; see main text for all details
column8: Achieved better balance between fidelity (PSNR) and perceptual quality (NRQM, PI), suppresses artifacts and hallucinations; recovers fine details with high reconstruction accuracy; visible improvements in regular patterns; competitive on both full-reference and no-reference metrics
column9: Introduces SWT-domain loss functions (fidelity and adversarial) to GAN-SR models; discriminator trained only on HF wavelet subbands; generator trained with subband-weighted SWT-domain loss; suppresses artifacts without explicit artifact maps; generic and easily pluggable framework; first to apply weighted wavelet-domain losses for SR in this way
column10: Consistent improvements on multiple datasets; demonstrates stability due to sparser input to discriminator; ablation studies show stable improvement with SWT domain losses; report of maintained or improved LR-consistency; effective on both visual and quantitative evaluations
column11: Quantitative measures do not always reflect suppression of artifacts; visual quality can exceed what is captured in PSNR/NRQM/PI; optimal loss weight determination for subbands is nontrivial and requires empirical search; PD trade-off point selection may require user adjustment; still room for improvement in discriminating details vs. artifacts
column12: Weighted wavelet-domain L1 (SWT fidelity loss), adversarial loss over HF subbands, DISTS perceptual loss (replacing conventional LPIPS/VGG), sum with tunable subband weights and coefficients; discriminator loss is standard GAN loss on HF subbands; see equations (1-4) in main text
column13: Supervised training (paired LR-HR images), transfer learning via pre-trained RRDB generator initialization

column16: Pooling/wavelet applied to Y-channel: SWT applied for both loss computation (generator) and discriminator input; losses calculated on each subband; for 2-level SWT, losses on all new subbands; discriminator operates only on HF SWT subbands (not RGB images); generator does not use wavelet in forward pass, just for loss calculations
column17: Direct comparison with ESRGAN-FS, ESRGAN+, SPSR, RankSRGAN, SRFlow-DA, LDL, FxSR, PDASR, DualFormer, SROOE; ablation studies show cumulative benefit of switching to DISTS, then SWT l1, then SWT adversarial losses; outperforming SOTA in NRQM, PI, maintaining PSNR; see Tables 1-2
column18: Training: 32x32 LR RGB patches; output resolution ×4 upsampled; benchmarks include up to HR 256×256 and higher depending on dataset
column19: Baselines: ESRGAN-FS, ESRGAN+, SPSR, RankSRGAN, SRFlow-DA, LDL, FxSR, PDASR, DualFormer, SROOE; see ablation #0 for ESRGAN baseline

column17: Direct comparison and ablation with seven existing LDCT denoising algorithms (BM3D, Red-CNN, WD-CNN, PWGAN-WSHL, f-GAN, RegNet-GAN, GAN-CIRCLE) and module ablations; PCCNN achieves top SSIM and strong PSNR.
column18: Patch size for training: 64×64 (from 512×512 images); full-size 512×512 for test images.
column19: Baselines: BM3D, Red-CNN, WD-CNN, PWGAN-WSHL, f-GAN, RegNet-GAN, GAN-CIRCLE; both supervised and unsupervised methods compared.
column20: Encoder-decoder with four encoding/decoding stages, residual blocks, bilinear upsampling, 4×4 convs, batch normalization, LeakyReLU, skip connections with CFFM + CIMA, attention modules with 3×3/1×1 convs, pooling in attention.
column22: Yes; combined AAPM and LDCT-and-projection-data for cross-dataset validation; also test set involved unpaired composition.
column23: Discusses memory efficiency, model trained on overlapping patches, batch size 16; MWT reduces learning burden; experiments performed on dual-GPU system with tests noted on scaling/ablation.
column24: Adam optimizer (β1=0.9, β2=0.999); initial LR=1e-4 decay to 1e-6.
column25: Ablation demonstrates effective regularization and stable convergence with all modules; loss curves analyzed; LeakyReLU activations; five-fold cross-validation; tuning of λ hyperparameters for best PSNR/SSIM.
column26: Feature maps separated into high- and low-frequency components via the wavelet transform; CFFM fuses spatial/frequency features; CIMA models channel/spatial dependencies; network focuses learning on noise/texture in high-frequency subbands.

column1: Unsupervised Generative Adversarial Network with Background Enhancement and Irredundant Pooling for Hyperspectral Anomaly Detection
column4: San Diego, Los Angeles, Texas Coast (TC-I, TC-II), Bay Champagne
column5: Two constrained GANs in spatial branch; AE-based generators with Leaky ReLU, PatchGAN-style discriminators
column6: Irredundant Pooling (IP; grouping max pooling in spectral branch to remove redundancy); background spatial feature enhancement (BE) in spatial branch
column7: AUC: San Diego: 0.9913, Los Angeles: 0.9478, TC-I: 0.9957, TC-II: 0.9984, Bay Champagne: 0.9998
column8: Achieved highest AUC values versus classics on most datasets; can detect anomalies with better spatial/shape fidelity; robust when anomaly region is large
column9: Proposes spatial-spectral joint architecture with (1) BE for stronger background-model learning, (2) irredundant pooling to fight redundancy, and (3) three-constraint loss for GAN stability
column10: Demonstrates improved stability via constraints (reconstruction, spectral angle, latent regularization); robust up to SNR >30dB noise; generalizes across several public HSI datasets
column11: Higher false alarm rates at boundary pixels and when anomalies are small or close to background; GAN instability partially persists; relatively high computational cost; not open-source
column12: Adversarial loss, reconstruction loss (L2), spectral angle constraint, latent L1 regularization
column13: Unsupervised
column14: Standard GAN (with two discriminator modifications); not cGAN/WGAN/LSGAN
column15: AE-based generator, PatchGAN-style discriminators (fully-connected layers with Leaky ReLU and Dropout; sizes described in paper)
column16: Pooling/wavelet: BE applied in spatial branch input (band grouping), IP applied in spectral branch before anomaly scoring (groupwise max).
column17: Comparisons: AUC versus RX, LRX, CRD, RPCA, GTVLRR, LRASR, LSMAD. BEGAIP achieved best or second-best across all datasets; ablation studies show importance of BE, IP, and constraints.
column18: Inputs: HSI cubes, experiments at 100x100 and 400x400 with 188-224 bands (subsampled)
column19: Rx, LRX, LRASR, CRD, GTVLRR, LSMAD, RPCA (as baselines)
column20: Layer stack: Each GAN has 3 fully connected layers in both generator and discriminator. Leaky ReLU activations, Dropout 0.1 in discriminators. PCA, morphological ops and guide filters in spatial postprocessing.
column22: Yes—evaluated on four public HSI datasets (cross-dataset)
column23: Processing scales with image size and bands (#params increases), slower than RX/CRD/RPCA; training time for spatial GAN substantial; full detection step (excl. training) ~10s per 100x100x200 HSI.
column24: Adam optimizer, learning rate 0.0001
column25: Training stability improved by three constraint losses (reconstruction, spectral angle, latent), epochs set via experiment for convergence; regularization for Mahalanobis in spectral branch
column26: Explicit spatial-spectral feature extraction; BE for background spatial features, IP for spectral maxima; ablation confirms both are critical for best performance.

column1: Visible-Image-Assisted Nonuniformity Correction of Infrared Images Using the GAN with SEBlock
column4: Public datasets: MSRS, M3FD, RoadScene; 5000 paired self-taken infrared-visible images (total 10,000 pairs with enhancement, split 6:2:2 for training/validation/testing)
column5: GAN with dual discriminators (VITD and DWTD), visible image assistance, SEBlock, multiscale encoding/decoding, skip connections
column6: Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) in discriminator for frequency-domain judgment; Max pooling (2×2) in encoder for downsampling; Global Average Pooling within SEBlock for channel attention
column7: Average SSIM >0.97, average PSNR 37.11 dB; >3% improvement over comparison methods; Table 2: SSIM 0.9661 and PSNR 37.1148 for proposed method
column8: Achieved strong noise removal while preserving textures and edges, outperforming baseline and state-of-the-art NUC methods; stable, robust corrections across varied input conditions
column9: Dual-discriminator architecture with vision transformer (VITD for spatial/texture, DWTD for frequency/noise), visible-channel SEBlock for contextual assistance, end-to-end correction for single-frame infrared NUC
column10: Extensively tested for stability (loss curves, ablation), robust to misregistration and occlusion in auxiliary visible inputs, generalizes well across datasets; SSIM and PSNR degrade little under occlusion/misregistration
column11: Correction performance slightly drops with large visible image occlusion; parallax/misalignment reduce auxiliary benefit; high computational resources needed for transformer-based discriminator; dataset dependence; future work on further generalization
column12: Adversarial loss, L1 loss, SSIM loss (weighted sum); separate adversarial losses for each discriminator; coefficients for L1 and SSIM both set to 100
column13: Supervised (paired IR-visible), end-to-end training
column14: Not explicitly stated; modeled as an enhanced GAN with dual discriminators (resembles conditional GAN structure for paired data)
column15: Generator: 11-layer encoder (Conv-BN-LeakyReLU-MaxPool), 6-layer decoder with skip connections and SEBlock, channel attention on visible feature maps. Discriminator: VITD (Vision Transformer+Residual+FC), DWTD (DWT+group conv+conv+pool+dropout+FC)
column16: Pooling in encoder (max pooling); global avg pooling within SEBlock; DWT at DWT-discriminator input (after generator output)
column17: Compares against BFTH, MHE, DMRN, Pix2Pix, RAN, CBDNet, DLS; demonstrates 0.03–0.12 boost in SSIM and ~2 dB in PSNR over best competitors; ablation studies for visible/SEBlock/dual-discriminator efficacy
column18: 256×256 images (scaled from 640×512 and visible image), 8-bit grayscale; DWT outputs 128×128×4 bands
column19: Baselines: BFTH, MHE, DMRN, Pix2Pix, RAN, CBDNet, DLS; also ablates GAN, Vit-GAN, Pix2Pix, and impact of visible/SEBlock features
column20: Encoder: Conv-BN-LeakyReLU-MaxPool; Decoder: Conv-BN-Deconv-BN-skip; SEBlock for channel attention; Discriminator: transformer encoders, group convolutions, residual connections, FC, dropout
column22: Validated on three public datasets (MSRS, M3FD, RoadScene) and self-taken images; also on data with parallax/occlusion and cross-condition settings
column23: Model can be trained stably on large sets (10,000 image pairs); VITD uses fewer layers to reduce transformer overhead; tested on hardware with RTX 3090, batch size 64; application to higher resolution possible but not directly addressed
column24: Adam optimizer, learning rate 0.0001, batch size 64
column25: Stable convergence, improved generalization (loss curves flat with VITD, rapid convergence); batch norm used for network stability; dropout to prevent discriminator overfitting; no GAN collapse observed
column26: Visible features: texture, structure, and edges from visible images extracted and mapped via SEBlock; generator fuses IR and visible features at each scale; DWT extracts frequency and orientation features in discriminator; added features increase corrected image fidelity and noise suppression

column1: W2GAN: Importance Weight and Wavelet feature guided Image-to-Image translation under limited data
column3: Image-to-image translation (multi-domain, especially under limited data)
column4: AFHQ, CelebA-HQ
column5: Custom GAN based on StarGAN v2 (baseline)
column6: Haar wavelet transform; Wavelet-AdaIN normalization applied in decoder (generator), adaptive fusion of high-frequency components from wavelet decomposition with features
column7: Latent-guided (AFHQ): FID 29.73, KID 0.0111, LPIPS 0.520; Reference-guided (AFHQ): FID 29.75, KID 0.0119, LPIPS 0.504; Latent-guided (CelebA-HQ): FID 26.67, KID 0.0172, LPIPS 0.494; Reference-guided (CelebA-HQ): FID 28.58, KID 0.0244, LPIPS 0.438
column8: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on FID and KID for I2I translation with limited data; Better synthesis of high-frequency content; Outperforms other SOTA methods in visual quality and diversity
column9: Introduces importance weighting in adversarial loss and Wavelet-AdaIN normalization for frequency-aware, high-quality I2I translation with small datasets; Proposes Threshold Pseudo Augmentation (TPA) to selectively augment data with high-quality generated samples
column10: Demonstrates improved stability and reduced over-fitting, stable under medium data sizes (N=300, N=500), effective at preventing training divergence, but performance drops with extremely small or very large datasets
column11: Fails to learn content/style when style/foreground is not clear; occasional species translation errors; performance disadvantage on full dataset compared to some baselines for latent-guided synthesis
column12: Adversarial loss with learnable importance weight, cycle consistency loss, style reconstruction loss, style diversification loss (weighted sum)
column13: Unsupervised (style and reference-guided translation; utilizes TPA data augmentation)
column14: cGAN (conditional GAN), compared against StarGAN v2, SmGAN, ReMix, SRIT
column15: Multi-branch discriminator with six downsampling residual blocks; generator with encoder (six down) and decoder (six up), residual units, IN/AdaIN, Wavelet-AdaIN normalization; style encoder (six pre-activation residual blocks), mapping network (MLP), auxiliary weight predictor
column16: Wavelet-AdaIN normalization applied within the decoder (generator); wavelet transform on content image features for high-frequency inputs in decoder
column17: Compared quantitatively and qualitatively to StarGAN v2, SmGAN, SRIT, ReMix; Ablation studies (TPA, importance weighting, wavelet fusion); Shows lower FID/KID than all baselines on limited data; qualitative examples provided
column19: StarGAN v2, SmGAN, ReMix, SRIT
column20: Conv-BN-ReLU stacks, pre-activation residual blocks in style encoder and discriminator, up/down-sampling, AdaIN/IN normalization, custom wavelet normalization, multi-branch outputs; mapping network is 4-layer MLP
column22: Tested on AFHQ and CelebA-HQ, but no explicit cross-dataset transfer results
column23: Runtimes not detailed; shows stable results for mid-sized limited data, but scalability/efficiency not deeply discussed
column24: Adam optimizer (β1=0, β2=0.99), different learning rates for modules (G, D, Es: 1e-4; M: 1e-6); batch size 8
column25: R1 regularization; importance weighting stabilizes adversarial loss; model stable for moderate data, struggles with extremely low data count; wavelet fusion eases convergence
column26: Wavelet decomposition for high-frequency components; features extracted as input to decoder and modulated within Wavelet-AdaIN for frequency-aware generation

column1: WSRGAN: A wavelet-based GAN for super-resolution of plane-wave ultrasound images without sampling loss
column4: PICMUS 2016 (Plane-wave Imaging Challenge in Medical UltraSound) dataset; Data augmentation with Gaussian noise and flipping, divided into 1350 training / 450 test images
column5: Conditional GAN (cGAN) with U-shaped encoder-decoder generator (U-Net variant)
column6: Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) and Inverse DWT (IDWT) replace pooling and upsampling; Haar wavelet used; Adaptive Wavelet Attention Module (AWAM) in the decoder
column7: Point target FWHM: 0.268 mm (sim) / 0.502 mm (exp); Cyst contrast: 37.223 dB (sim) / 26.156 dB (exp); Invivo PSNR: 41.518 dB, SSIM: 0.984; Outperforms baselines in all categories
column8: Achieved significant super-resolution of single-beam PW ultrasound; Improved contrast, SNR, and structural detail preservation; Outperformed SoTA/baselines in PSNR and SSIM
column9: Introduced wavelet-based (DWT/IDWT) no-loss sampling, adaptive wavelet attention, and novel Wavelet GAN loss; Multi-scale/hierarchical feature representation without data discard
column10: Demonstrated stable convergence, effective generalization across simulated, experimental, and in-vivo data; Ablation studies confirm robustness of modules; Handles augmentation noise well
column11: Limitations due to reliance on CPWC-75 ground truth for supervision, computational complexity (FLOPS), and lack of improvement beyond ground truth; Calls for unsupervised or semi-supervised extensions and model compression
column12: Wavelet GAN Loss (BCE + EM in wavelet domain); Combined generator loss: adversarial (Wavelet GAN), SSIM loss, perceptual loss
column13: Supervised training using paired LR/HR images; Data augmentation with noise and flipping; 8:2 train/test split
column15: U-shaped encoder–decoder generator (detailed Conv stacks); Discriminator uses paired real/fake composite Patch inputs
column16: DWT replaces pooling in encoder; IDWT replaces upsampling in decoder; AWAM attention modules in decoder at shallow and deep stages
column17: Compared with SISR models (SRGAN, VDSR, ResGAN, UnetGAN), classical beamforming (CF, GCF, MV), recent SoTA (AUGAN, Goudarzi et al., Nguon & Park), and with various ablations; WSRGAN outperforms all baselines/SoTA on PSNR/SSIM for invivo; Ablation validates wavelet and attention design
column18: Input size not explicitly specified; Dataset images derived from PICMUS, typical for US imaging, resolution not detailed
column19: SRGAN, VDSR, ResGAN, UnetGAN, CPWC, CF, GCF, MV, AUGAN, CID-Net, and other listed SoTA
column20: Encoder: 5 Conv+ReLU layers with DWT between each; Decoder: 4 Conv+ReLU layers with IDWT and AWAM; Skip-connections; Patch-based discriminator
column22: No mention of explicit cross-dataset testing; Only within simulated, experimental, invivo subsets of PICMUS
column23: 148.48 billion FLOPS and 42.38M parameters; Comparable parameter size to Unet-based models; Slower than AUGAN but practical for modern GPUs; No mention of scaling to higher res/different modalities
column24: Adam for generator, RMSProp for discriminator; Learning rate 0.002; ReduceLROnPlateau for scheduling
column25: Stable convergence verified via ablation/metric analysis; Regularization via attentive and multi-scale feature fusion; GAN and perceptual/SSIM losses promote convergence; Model robust to augmentations
column26: Features extracted at multiple scales/frequencies using Haar DWT; AWAM specializes deep/shallow frequency attention; Generator preserves both global and texture details explicitly in frequency and spatial domain

column1: Detection of GAN-Synthesized Image Based on Discrete Wavelet Transform
column4: StyleGAN2-synthesized fake faces; CelebA (real), plus benchmark images by StarGAN, PGGAN, BigGAN, CycleGAN, GauGAN, PixelRNN, DiscoGAN
column5: Not applicable (discriminative/classification baseline)
column6: Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT), applied separately to RGB channels; level-1 DWT used for feature extraction
column7: On StyleGAN2 faces: Precision 0.9845, Recall 0.9845, F1 0.9845, Accuracy 0.9845, AP 0.9889, AUC 0.9901, FPR 0.021, FNR 0.015; Robustness AUC >0.8 under compression/resizing, >0.95 with blur/noise; On other GANs (AP): StyleGAN 85.33, PGGAN 76.32, BigGAN 75.10, CycleGAN 96.19, StarGAN 99.71, GauGAN 91.22, PixelRNN 90.33, DiscoGAN 95.23
column8: Model distinguishes GAN-synthesized from real images with high accuracy and is robust to multiple perturbation attacks; effective across types of GAN-generated content.
column9: Novel DWT-based spectral correlation feature between RGB channels for real vs. GAN image discrimination; uses kurtosis over inter-channel wavelet correlation matrices.
column10: Demonstrated robustness to compression, blur, resizing, noise, and effective across a range of GAN architectures and content types. Maintains performance under multiple perturbations.
column11: Lower performance for detecting multi-type or more complex GAN-generated images; relatively less effective for challenging high-quality or very diverse fakes (e.g., BigGAN/PGGAN/StyleGAN); detection of multi-type fakes and DeepFakes left as future work.
column12: SVM with radial basis function (RBF) kernel; uses kurtosis of DWT-based spectral correlation; no GAN-related loss since not a generative model.
column13: Supervised (SVM trained with labeled real/fake images)
column15: Not applicable
column16: Pooling/wavelet: Level-1 DWT applied to RGB channels of input images, prior to feature calculation.
column17: Compared against AutoGAN and FakeSpotter on multiple metrics (FID outperforms both overall and under perturbations; see Table 2 and Table 3 for figures).
column18: Input size not explicitly specified; CelebA images and GAN fakes (commonly 256×256 or 1024×1024), but Not specified" for this study."
column19: Baselines: AutoGAN, FakeSpotter (for StyleGAN2 faces); previous standard detection techniques for other datasets.
column20: DWT for feature extraction, SVM for classification. Feature vector: kurtosis over 3 correlation matrices from DWT between RGB subbands. No deep network/Conv layers described.
column21: Code partially available (datasets listed at https://github.com/NVlabs/stylegan2 and https://github.com/peterwang512/CNNDetection); source code to be uploaded to GitHub or available by request.
column22: Evaluated on multiple GAN datasets distinct from each other; tested on images synthesized by various GANs not used for SVM training.
column23: Discussion of scalability to more diverse datasets; indicates slight drop in performance as image content variety increases; computationally lightweight (DWT + low-dim feature + SVM).
column24: Not specified; SVM optimization uses grid search for RBF kernel hyperparameter.
column25: Training stable due to simple features and SVM; grid search for SVM regularization; no adversarial/generative training required.
column26: DWT-based inter-channel (RGB) frequency correlation features, specifically kurtosis of correlation coefficient matrices from DWT subbands, found effective for discriminating GAN fakes.

column1: Hyperspectral Image Classification Based on Transformer and Generative Adversarial Network
column4: Indian Pines, Pavia University
column5: TransGAN (Transformer-based GAN)
column6: Multi-scale pooling module (MSPM) applied in discriminator; no wavelet transform
column8: Achieved state-of-the-art accuracy on Indian Pines and Pavia University datasets, especially robust on minority classes.
column9: Combining a Transformer-based generator with a multi-scale pooling module in the discriminator to improve class-wise balance and reduce mode collapse; introduces SPCA (superpixelwise PCA) for efficient feature extraction.
column10: Demonstrated robust performance on both datasets, especially on minority classes; alleviates mode collapse and imbalance issues.
column11: Future work includes aiming for higher classification accuracy; potential drawbacks not deeply analyzed.
column12: Log-likelihood/classification loss functions; see Eq. (3) and (4) for details.
column13: Supervised training; adversarial learning.
column15: Generator: Transformer encoder with upsampling and pixelshuffle modules; Discriminator: 5-layer CNN with MSPM
column16: MSPM applied in discriminator after convolutions; SPCA used for preprocessing before main network.
column17: Compared to 1D-CNN, 3D-CNN, HybridSN, Multiscale 3D-CNN, ADGAN; SPCA-TransGAN achieved best OA/AA/Kappa.
column18: Input patches ultimately reach 64×64×3 in the generation process; Indian Pines data is 145×145, Pavia University is 610×340; main experiments use 64×64 patches.
column19: 1D-CNN, 3D-CNN, HybridSN, Multiscale 3D-CNN, ADGAN
column20: Conv-BN-LeakyReLU in discriminator; residual connections, multi-head self-attention, feed-forward MLP, pixelshuffle and upsampling in generator.
column22: Tested on both datasets but not explicitly on unseen, out-of-domain datasets.
column23: Lowered computational complexity via SPCA and Transformer upsampling; aims for efficient training but large-scale or high-res claims not detailed.
column24: Adam optimizer (initial lr=0.0001, decay=0.99, batch size=5)
column25: Stable convergence reported, losses monitored per epoch, lowest-loss model selected; regularization via SPCA and adversarial training.
column26: SPCA for superpixelwise PCA feature extraction; generator learns spatial-spectral features via Transformer encoder, discriminator exploits multi-scale features with MSPM.

column1: Image Derain Method for Generative Adversarial Network Based on Wavelet High Frequency Feature Fusion
column3: Image deraining (removal of rain streaks from images)
column4: Common rain streaks dataset (includes sparse and dense rain streaks); each with 4400 pairs (4000 training, 400 testing)

column8: Demonstrated improved rain removal on both sparse and dense datasets with better background preservation and reduced rain streaks compared to baselines
column9: Introduces wavelet-based high frequency feature extraction and fusion, combined with self-attention and multi-scale discriminator for enhanced deraining
column10: Shows faster loss convergence, higher PSNR/SSIM than baselines, and improved visual and quantitative results in ablation and comparison experiments
column11: Requires GPU due to complexity and large parameter count, generalization beyond provided datasets not reported; limited information on scalability to real-world scenarios
column12: Adversarial loss, multiscale loss (mean squared error), perceptual loss (VGG-based), attention loss
column13: Supervised
column14: cGAN (conditioning via attention and feature fusion); based on AttGAN improvements

column18: Not specified
column19: Eigen, EfDeRain, F-S GAN, R-IPM CA, AttGAN
column20: Conv-ReLU stacks, Squeeze-and-Excitation modules in fusion, self-attention, multi-scale discriminator, jump connections
column22: Not specified
column23: Discusses need for GPU due to complexity, but no explicit analysis of scalability to higher-res datasets
column24: Not specified; code uses TensorFlow 1.12, but optimizer not named

column26: Wavelet high-frequency features extracted by DB2 transform and fused; self-attention enhances global dependencies; explicit focus on frequency-domain features for highlighting rain locations

column1: SHBGAN: Hybrid Bilateral Attention GAN for Seismic Image Super-Resolution Reconstruction
column4: Synthetic seismic dataset (generated with diverse faults and noise) and Netherlands F3 field dataset
column5: Hybrid Bilateral Attention GAN (SHBGAN, GAN-based)
column6: No wavelet transform used; global average pooling and global max pooling (GAP/GMP) in attention modules within generator and as pooling in discriminator
column7: PSNR: >36 dB (synthetic); SHBGAN outperforms U-Net and SRGAN by ~6 dB. SSIM best among compared methods. (Field PSNR/SSIM not explicitly reported).
column8: Significantly enhances resolution, recovers weak signals and subtle structures (such as faults), robust for both synthetic and field seismic images.
column9: Hybrid and bilateral attention modules (BAMs/HAB); dilated convolution replaces all BN layers in residual blocks; GAN discriminator outputs scalar authenticity score (not probability); custom loss with MSSIM.
column10: Demonstrated robustness on field data and unseen dataset (New Zealand); ablation and cross-validation show strong generalization and stability; architecture changes (removing BN, adding BAM/HAB) improve robustness.
column11: Deficiencies remain when field data are heavily impacted by strong noise; strong reliance on paired data for supervised training.
column12: L1 loss, adversarial loss, MSSIM loss (weighted sum: L1 + α*adv + β*MSSIM, α=1e-3, β=1e-4); BCE loss for discriminator.
column13: Supervised training on paired low/high-resolution data; training/validation split 8:2; transfer/generalization tested by direct application to other survey area.
column14: Standard GAN (no mention of cGAN/LSGAN/WGAN variants); compared with other GANs (SRGAN, SeisGAN, ESRGAN).
column15: Generator: Residual network with dilated convolutions, HAB and BAM, depthwise separable convolutions for upsampling; Discriminator: convolutional layers with global average pooling, outputs scalar authenticity.
column16: Global average pooling and global max pooling used in generator (CAM, SAM, BAM) and discriminator; pooling after feature maps for dimensionality reduction and attention; not wavelet-based, no pooling in encoder/decoder heads.
column17: Comparisons to U-Net, SRGAN, ESRGAN, SeisGAN, and ablated SHBGAN variants; SHBGAN superior in all metrics and fields; ablation validates contributions of BAM, HAB, and removal of BN.
column18: Input size for training: 128×128 (LR) upsampled to 256×256 (HR); experiments on field data at higher, unspecified resolutions.
column19: U-Net, SRGAN, ESRGAN, SeisGAN, and ablated SHBGAN models.
column20: Conv layers with ReLU, depthwise separable conv, residual blocks with dilated convolutions; attention modules (CAM, SAM, S-A); no BN layers; FC in attention, not in discriminator.
column22: Generalization tested via New Zealand seismic data (not seen in training); SHBGAN shows strong cross-dataset transfer.
column23: Model designed for enhanced generalization and reduced computational complexity (dilated conv over BN); depthwise separable conv in upsampling for efficiency; scalability discussed via applicability to field data, not explicit numbers.
column24: Adam optimizer (initial lr: 2e-4), cosine learning rate decay, momentum 0.9, no weight decay; batch size 8, >200 epochs.
column25: Training is stable (especially with scalar output discriminator and loss balancing); removal of BN layers and use of MSSIM in loss aids convergence and avoids overfitting; weight analysis for loss confirms stability.
column26: Emphasis on extracting local/global features (HAB/BAM); attention modules enhance important seismic events (fault/weak signals); frequency domain features (amplified mid/high bands) verified via spectrum analysis.

column1: Creating Realistic Anterior Segment Optical Coherence Tomography Images using Generative Adversarial Networks
column3: Medical imaging, synthetic image generation, OCT image augmentation
column4: 142,628 AS-OCT B-scans from AUBMC (10-mm and 16-mm width scans)
column5: SWAGAN (Style and Wavelet-based GAN), plus ESRGAN for super-resolution
column6: Wavelet-driven spectral convolution within SWAGAN; applied in generator (frequency domain); ESRGAN for super-resolution upscaling
column7: FID (16-mm: 5.12, 10-mm: 7.60, Real: 1.10); CNN accuracy: real: 78%, synthetic: 76%, combined: 100%; LPIPS (SR): 10-mm: 0.0847, 16-mm: 0.0962 (bicubic worse: 0.1167, 0.7320)
column8: SWAGAN generated highly realistic AS-OCT scans indistinguishable from real images; augmented data improved downstream task accuracy; ESRGAN upscaled images to 1024×1024 with superior quality to interpolation.
column9: Application of wavelet-driven StyleGAN2 (SWAGAN) for AS-OCT images and demonstration of closed-form factorization for unsupervised image editing; use of ESRGAN for resolution enhancement in medical imaging.
column10: Extensive evaluation: low FID; experts could not reliably distinguish synthetic from real images (accuracy near chance); tested model generalizability on diverse AS-OCT pathology; robust super-resolution via ESRGAN.
column11: Image resolution limited to 512×512 before super-resolution; GAN-generated pathologies may not match real-world corneal structure; latent vector edits can entangle other features; some unrealistic artifacts observed.
column12: Non-saturating adversarial loss with R1 regularization for discriminator, path length regularization for generator; perceptual loss (ESRGAN/LPIPS) for super-resolution.
column13: Unsupervised training for SWAGAN, supervised fine-tuning for ESRGAN; downstream CNN tested in supervised way.
column14: StyleGAN2 variant: SWAGAN (for generation); ESRGAN (for super-resolution)
column15: SWAGAN generator (style-based with wavelet spectral convolutions), discriminator unspecified; ESRGAN uses its standard networks.
column16: Wavelet spectral convolutions in SWAGAN generator; upscaling via ESRGAN decoder; pooling not highlighted
column17: Compared FID, LPIPS, and visual quality to bicubic, bilinear, nearest neighbor, and Lanczos upsampling; synthetic data evaluated vs real for CNN classification (synthetic data improved accuracy to 100%)
column18: Input images resized to 512×512; upsampled to 1024×1024 via ESRGAN; patch size 128×128 for super-resolution
column19: Compared against traditional upsampling (bicubic, bilinear, Lanczos, nearest); compared CNN trained on real vs synthetic vs mixed data
column20: Conv layers with wavelet spectral convolutions in generator; PyTorch implementation; ESRGAN for super-resolution; training over 2 RTX 3080 GPUs
column22: Fleiss' kappa for inter-expert agreement; dataset diverse, but no explicit cross-dataset; real/test split for CNN evaluation
column23: Training over 500,000 iterations, batch size 4, 2 GPUs; scalability discussed in augmentation context; upsampling to 1024x1024
column24: Adam optimizer (learning rate 0.002) for GANs and ESRGAN
column25: Path length regularization (generator); non-saturating loss with R1 reg (discriminator); lowest FID model selection; visual/manual artifact checks; checkpointing every 10,000 steps
column26: Latent vector manipulation (closed-form factorization) enables image editing (eyelid, artifacts, corneal changes); feature learning in frequency domain via wavelet convolutions

column1: Data Generation Methods of Generative Adversarial Networks Based on Exponential Adaptive Pool
column3: Image synthesis (high-resolution image generation with small samples)
column4: Panda, Anime, Dog, Obama
column5: FastGAN (EAP-FastGAN variant)
column6: Exponential Adaptive Pool (AdaPool); replaces average pooling in both generator and discriminator, fusing weighted feature maps based on pixel distances and values
column7: FID: Panda 10.85, Anime 58.33, Dog 78.18, Obama 44.85 (EAP-FastGAN) vs. FastGAN: Panda 11.57, Anime 59.02, Dog 78.27, Obama 45.63
column8: EAP-FastGAN improves image quality and stability, outperforming FastGAN in all tested datasets (lower FID, more stable loss).
column9: Introduction of exponential adaptive pooling (AdaPool) in both generator and discriminator for better feature retention and stability in small-sample and high-resolution image generation.
column10: EAP-FastGAN demonstrates improved stability and generalization during training, especially visible in quantified loss function plots and FID metrics across datasets.
column11: Model still limited in generating ultra high-resolution images; future work needed for higher resolutions.
column12: Adversarial loss (hinge loss); self-supervised reconstruction loss in discriminator training.
column13: Supervised (using paired real and generated images for evaluation, identical training/validation sets).
column15: Self-supervised discriminator (encoder-decoder, adaptive exponentially weighted pooling); lightweight generator with skip-layer excitation and channel-level excitation modules.
column16: AdaPool replaces all average pooling in generator and discriminator; skip-layer excitation (SLE) module in generator uses AdaPool for feature recalibration.
column17: Compared directly with original FastGAN using FID and loss convergence curves; EAP-FastGAN consistently outperforms FastGAN.
column18: 256×256 (Panda, Dog, Obama), 512×512 (Anime)
column19: FastGAN
column20: Conv-BatchNorm-GLU stacks, residual-like skip-layer channel-wise excitation, adaptive pooling modules, lightweight convolutional design.
column22: No explicit cross-dataset generalization analysis conducted.
column23: AdaPool introduces only negligible additional computation; model remains lightweight and fast while generating high-res images from small samples.
column24: Adam optimizer (implied standard for Pytorch-based GANs, but not explicitly stated)
column25: AdaPool increases stability and convergence speed; stable loss curves and improved simultaneous generator/discriminator optimization.
column26: AdaPool enables retention of both global and fine image details by fusing features based on distance and value; feature extraction focuses on preserving texture and detail signals.
